title: Unit 31 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Ancient Remnants & Formal Registers

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 43: Ancient Remnants. This is our final deep dive before the finish line. Even though we speak Modern Greek, the shadows of the ancient language are everywhere—in our law, our church, and our formal greetings. I'm Ava, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... is it true that some Greeks still speak like Plato in certain situations?

Γεια σας! Not exactly like Plato, but we use 'Kath-a-ré-vou-sa' remnants. This was a 19th-century version of Greek that tried to bridge the gap between ancient and modern. It was the official language until 1976! So today, we have many 'fossils' in our daily speech.
The artifacts of the 19th century. Let's look at some phrases that every Greek student knows, but might not realize are ancient. Like 'En tá-xei'.

'En tá-xei' means 'Okay' or 'In order'. It uses the ancient Dative case, which officially doesn't exist in Modern Greek! The word 'En' means 'In', and 'tá-xei' is the dative of 'tá-xis'. We use it every single day.
'En tá-xei'. It sounds so modern, but it’s actually a grammatical ghost! What about 'Dó-xa to The-ó'?
'Glory to God'. Again, 'to The-ó' is the Dative case. In Modern Greek, we would say 'ston The-ó'. But for religious or traditional phrases, we keep the ancient form. It sounds more... respectful.
It adds a layer of solemnity. We also see this in formal verbs. Verbs that start with 'Pro-'. Like 'Pro-er-khó-ma-stee' (we originate from).

Exactly. Many 'high-level' verbs used in news and books are very ancient. 'E-pe-dée' (because), 'Me-tá' combined with different cases... these all come from that formal bridge. If you want to work in a Greek office, you must learn these remnants.

Pro Tip: Think of these phrases like 'Latinisms' in English. Like saying 'Et cetera' or 'Ad hoc'. They aren't the base of the language anymore, but they are the decorations of an educated speaker. Athina, are there any ancient remnants that are actually... funny?
Maybe 'I-doú i Ró-dos' which we mentioned in Unit 33! Or 'Pýr kai ma-née-a'... fire and fury. We use it when someone is extremely angry. It sounds very dramatic, like a scene from an ancient tragedy!

Drama and history. That is the essence of Greece. By learning these remnants, you aren't just learning a language... you are connecting with three thousand years of human thought. Excellent work today.
Σας συγχαίρω! You have reached a levels of understanding that few students ever do. Γεια σας!
This concludes Unit 43. We have navigated the past to master the present. We’ll see you soon. Ya sas!
